Former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | Founded=Traditionally: Year 6 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōan|Q312821|WD=Q312821|1=Emperor Kōan}} ({{ill|lt=386 BCE|Q228485|WD=Q228485|1=386 BC}}) | Main hall= | Annual festival=Third Sunday of October | Map = {{Location map|Japan Nara|label=Katte Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} '''Katte Shrine''' (かつてじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Yoshino District|Q1154558|WD=Q1154558|1=Yoshino District, Nara}}, {{ill|lt=Yoshino Town|Q1203731|WD=Q1203731|1=Yoshino, Nara}}. According to the *{{ill|lt=Wakan Sansai Zue|Q1135803|WD=Q1135803|1=Wakan Sansai Zue}}*, the enshrined deity is described as “Katte-sha: one enshrined deity, Aiman-no-Mikoto{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2569751 | title=Wakan Sansai Zue: 105 volumes, front 1 volume, end 1 volume [55] | work=National Diet Library Digital Collection | accessdate=2018-10-27}} (Ukeri-no-Mikoto / Ukenori-no-Mikoto).” It is also written as “Ukeman-no-Mikoto”{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=668 | title=Shrine Introduction > Shrines of Shiga Prefecture | work=Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines | accessdate=2018-10-27}}. The current principal enshrined deity is said to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōyama-tsumi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Kukunochi|Q10749920|WD=Q10749920|lt=Kukunochi-no-Mikoto|1=Kukunochi}}, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Koke-mushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hano-hime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} are also enshrined together. === Overview === It is one of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshino Hassha Myōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}, guardian shrines of {{ill|lt=Mount Ōmine|Q3695989|WD=Q3695989|1=Mount Ōmine}} in Yoshino, and was formerly called “'''Katte Myōjin'''.” {{ill|lt=Aonegamine|Q11661968|WD=Q11661968|1=Mount Aonegamine}}, which corresponds to the source of the {{ill|lt=Yoshino River (Nara Prefecture)|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshino River}}, has been revered since ancient times as a {{ill|lt=water deity|Q3137449|WD=Q3137449|1=Suijin}}. Accordingly, {{ill|lt=Kinpusen Shrine (Yoshino Town)|Q3197197|WD=Q3197197|lt=Kinpusen Shrine|1=Kinpu Shrine (Q3197197)}} was built near the summit (Oku-Senbon), {{ill|lt=Yoshino Mikumari Shrine|Q3529714|WD=Q3529714|1=Yoshino Mikumari Shrine}} on the mountainside (Kami-Senbon), and Katte Shrine at the mountain’s foot (Naka-Senbon)“The History of Popular Salvation and Buddhism (Vol. 2)” by Munetoshi Nakaya, Ikuhōsha, 2012. “Katte” is also said to mean “entrance” or “lower side,” and from its written form it came to be worshipped as a deity of contests and as a {{ill|lt=war god|Q11637440|WD=Q11637440|lt=god of battle|1=Military god}}. During the period of {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the honji (original Buddhist identity) of Katte Daimyōjin was said to be {{ill|lt=Bishamonten|Q866315|WD=Q866315|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}, leading to even greater veneration by the warrior class. In addition, because it is located at the entrance to {{ill|lt=Mount Yoshino|Q1036938|WD=Q1036938|1=Mount Yoshino}}, it was also called Yamaguchi Shrine“Nara: A Grown-Up’s Walking Guide,” Seibido Publishing, 2010. The date of foundation is unknown, but the *Nichiyūji Keitōki* records it as the 6th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōan|Q312821|WD=Q312821|1=Emperor Kōan}} ({{ill|lt=386 BCE|Q228485|WD=Q228485|1=386 BC}}). It is said that when {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181|lt=Prince Ōama|1=Emperor Tenmu}} played the koto in the shrine hall, {{ill|lt=celestial maidens|Q111467505|WD=Q111467505|1=Celestial maiden}} descended and danced, waving their sleeves five times. The mountain behind the shrine came to be called “Sodefuri-yama” (Sleeve-Waving Mountain). This legend is also said to be the origin of the court dance known as “{{ill|lt=Gosechi no Mai|Q11372604|WD=Q11372604|1=Gosechi no Mai}}”. Within the precincts remains a dance mound said to mark the spot where {{ill|lt=Shizuka Gozen|Q3088217|WD=Q3088217|1=Shizuka Gozen}}, the wife of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Q310445|WD=Q310445|1=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}}, performed a dance when she was captured by pursuers. The {{ill|lt=main hall|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}}, built in the {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style with a {{ill|lt=hiwada-buki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} roof, measuring eight bays by two bays, was designated a prefectural {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}}. However, it was destroyed by suspected arson in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}). The sacred object has since been transferred to the facing {{ill|lt=Yoshimizu Shrine|Q3572667|WD=Q3572667|1=Yoshimizu Shrine}}, and donations are being solicited for the reconstruction of the main hall. === Relationship with Zao Gongen and Komori Myōjin === {{Multiple image | footer = Katte Myōjin (left) and Komori Myōjin (right) as depicted in the Yoshino Mandala | image1 = Katte-Myojin.jpg | image2 = Komori-Myojin.jpg | total_width = 350 }} {{ill|lt=Zao Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}} ({{ill|lt=Kinpusen-ji|Q1741668|WD=Q1741668|1=Kimpusen-ji}}), Komori Gongen (Yoshino Mikumari Shrine), and Katte Gongen (Katte Shrine) were enshrined together as the Three Gongen at {{ill|lt=Mount Mitoku’s Sanbutsu-ji|Q1053703|WD=Q1053703|lt=Sanbutsu-ji in Hōki Province|1=Hōki Province}}. Zao Gongen was enshrined at the inner sanctuary ({{ill|lt=Nageire-dō|Q19826408|WD=Q19826408|1=Nageire-dō}}), Komori Gongen at the Jizō Hall, and Katte Gongen at the Monju Hall{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2007-12-26| url=https://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/224955/02-1bunkazaiseirihyou.pdf | title=“Proposal for Assets Listed on the Tentative List of World Heritage Sites”: Cultural Properties Included in the Assets (5-1) Inventory Table | work=Official Tottori Prefecture Website | accessdate=2013-04-15}}. Katte Myōjin was also enshrined independently at shrines throughout the country and serves as the head shrine of 28 Katte Shrines nationwide{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com/pdf/syuisho.pdf | title=Prospectus for Support of Katte Shrine Reconstruction | work=Official Website of Yoshimizu Shrine | accessdate=2013-04-10|archive-url=https://www.web.archive.org/web/20060219194625/http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com:80/pdf/syuisho.pdf |archive-date=2006-02-19 |url-status=dead |url-status-date=2024-09-21}}. Komori Myōjin, the enshrined deity of Yoshino Mikumari Shrine, is said to be the consort deity of Katte Myōjin, with Katte Myōjin as the male deity and Komori Myōjin as the female deity. In the Noh play “{{ill|lt=Arashiyama (Noh)|Q17988500|WD=Q17988500|lt=Arashiyama|1=Arashiyama}},” which was established in the late Muromachi period, an elderly couple who serve as guardians of the cherry blossoms of {{ill|lt=Arashiyama|Q2859566|WD=Q2859566|1=Arashiyama}}—transplanted from Yoshino—are revealed to be manifestations of the deities Katte and Komori. The play’s plot proclaims that Zao Gongen, Katte Myōjin, and Komori Myōjin are three aspects of a single divine entity{{Cite web || url=http://www.tessen.org/dictionary/explain/arashiyama/ | title=Arashiyama | work=Tessenkai Noh Theatre Dictionary | accessdate=2013-04-09}}. {{-}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Katte-jinja (Yoshino, Nara)}} {{Osm box|n|2725147868}} * [https://www.kattejinja.com/ Katte Shrine Reconstruction Committee] {{Shinto}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Nara Prefecture}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Katte Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Katte Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Katte Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:奈良県の神社]] [[Category:奈良県の旧村社]] [[Category:大和国の式内社|大かつて]] [[Category:吉野町の建築物]] [[Category:吉野町の歴史]] [[Category:吉野・大峯]] [[Category:pages]]

It is said that when {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181|lt=Prince Ōama|1=Emperor Tenmu}} played the koto in the shrine hall, {{ill|lt=celestial maidens|Q111467505|WD=Q111467505|1=Celestial maiden}} descended and danced, waving their sleeves five times. The mountain behind the shrine came to be called “Sodefuri-yama” (Sleeve-Waving Mountain). Within the precincts remains a dance mound said to mark the spot where {{ill|lt=Shizuka Gozen|Q3088217|WD=Q3088217|1=Shizuka Gozen}}, the wife of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Q310445|WD=Q310445|1=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}}, performed a dance when she was captured by pursuers. The {{ill|lt=main hall|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}}, built in the {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style with a {{ill|lt=hiwada-buki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} roof, measuring eight bays by two bays, was designated a prefectural {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}}. However, it was destroyed by suspected arson in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}). The sacred object has since been transferred to the facing {{ill|lt=Yoshimizu Shrine|Q3572667|WD=Q3572667|1=Yoshimizu Shrine}}, and donations are being solicited for the reconstruction of the main hall. === Relationship with Zao Gongen and Komori Myōjin === {{Multiple image | footer = Katte Myōjin (left) and Komori Myōjin (right) as depicted in the Yoshino Mandala | image1 = Katte-Myojin.jpg | image2 = Komori-Myojin.jpg | total_width = 350 }} {{ill|lt=Zao Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}} ({{ill|lt=Kinpusen-ji|Q1741668|WD=Q1741668|1=Kimpusen-ji}}), Komori Gongen (Yoshino Mikumari Shrine), and Katte Gongen (Katte Shrine) were enshrined together as the Three Gongen at {{ill|lt=Mount Mitoku’s Sanbutsu-ji|Q1053703|WD=Q1053703|lt=Sanbutsu-ji in Hōki Province|1=Hōki Province}}. Zao Gongen was enshrined at the inner sanctuary ({{ill|lt=Nageire-dō|Q19826408|WD=Q19826408|1=Nageire-dō}}), Komori Gongen at the Jizō Hall, and Katte Gongen at the Monju Hall{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2007-12-26| url=https://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/224955/02-1bunkazaiseirihyou.pdf | title=“Proposal for Assets Listed on the Tentative List of World Heritage Sites”: Cultural Properties Included in the Assets (5-1) Inventory Table | work=Official Tottori Prefecture Website | accessdate=2013-04-15}}. Katte Myōjin was also enshrined independently at shrines throughout the country and serves as the head shrine of 28 Katte Shrines nationwide{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com/pdf/syuisho.pdf | title=Prospectus for Support of Katte Shrine Reconstruction | work=Official Website of Yoshimizu Shrine | accessdate=2013-04-10|archive-url=https://www.web.archive.org/web/20060219194625/http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com:80/pdf/syuisho.pdf |archive-date=2006-02-19 |url-status=dead |url-status-date=2024-09-21}}. Komori Myōjin, the enshrined deity of Yoshino Mikumari Shrine, is said to be the consort deity of Katte Myōjin, with Katte Myōjin as the male deity and Komori Myōjin as the female deity. In the Noh play “{{ill|lt=Arashiyama (Noh)|Q17988500|WD=Q17988500|lt=Arashiyama|1=Arashiyama}},” which was established in the late Muromachi period, an elderly couple who serve as guardians of the cherry blossoms of {{ill|lt=Arashiyama|Q2859566|WD=Q2859566|1=Arashiyama}}—transplanted from Yoshino—are revealed to be manifestations of the deities Katte and Komori. The play’s plot proclaims that Zao Gongen, Katte Myōjin, and Komori Myōjin are three aspects of a single divine entity{{Cite web || url=http://www.tessen.org/dictionary/explain/arashiyama/ | title=Arashiyama | work=Tessenkai Noh Theatre Dictionary | accessdate=2013-04-09}}. {{-}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Katte-jinja (Yoshino, Nara)}} {{Osm box|n|2725147868}} * [https://www.kattejinja.com/ Katte Shrine Reconstruction Committee] {{Shinto}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Nara Prefecture}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Katte Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Katte Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:奈良県の神社]] [[Category:奈良県の旧村社]] [[Category:大和国の式内社|大かつて]] [[Category:吉野町の建築物]] [[Category:吉野町の歴史]] [[Category:吉野・大峯]] [[Category:pages]]

in Yoshino Town, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Nara City|Katte Shrine (Nara City)}} {{Shrine | Image= [[File:Katte-jinja Yoshino Nara01n4272.jpg|250px]]
Torii gate | Name=Katte Shrine | Location={{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yoshino District|Q1154558|WD=Q1154558|1=Yoshino District, Nara}}{{ill|lt=Yoshino Town|Q1203731|WD=Q1203731|1=Yoshino, Nara}} Yoshinoyama 2354 | Coordinates={{Coord|34|21|52.9|N|135|51|44.9|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deity={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} | Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (major) disputed shrine
Former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | Founded=Traditionally: Year 6 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōan|Q312821|WD=Q312821|1=Emperor Kōan}} ({{ill|lt=386 BCE|Q228485|WD=Q228485|1=386 BC}}) | Main hall= | Annual festival=Third Sunday of October | Map = {{Location map|Japan Nara|label=Katte Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} '''Katte Shrine''' (かつてじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Yoshino District|Q1154558|WD=Q1154558|1=Yoshino District, Nara}}, {{ill|lt=Yoshino Town|Q1203731|WD=Q1203731|1=Yoshino, Nara}}. According to the *{{ill|lt=Wakan Sansai Zue|Q1135803|WD=Q1135803|1=Wakan Sansai Zue}}*, the enshrined deity is described as “Katte-sha: one enshrined deity, Aiman-no-Mikoto{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2569751 | title=Wakan Sansai Zue: 105 volumes, front 1 volume, end 1 volume [55] | work=National Diet Library Digital Collection | accessdate=2018-10-27}} (Ukeri-no-Mikoto / Ukenori-no-Mikoto).” It is also written as “Ukeman-no-Mikoto”{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=668 | title=Shrine Introduction > Shrines of Shiga Prefecture | work=Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines | accessdate=2018-10-27}}. The current principal enshrined deity is said to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōyama-tsumi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Kukunochi|Q10749920|WD=Q10749920|lt=Kukunochi-no-Mikoto|1=Kukunochi}}, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Koke-mushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hano-hime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} are also enshrined together. === Overview === It is one of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshino Hassha Myōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}, guardian shrines of {{ill|lt=Mount Ōmine|Q3695989|WD=Q3695989|1=Mount Ōmine}} in Yoshino, and was formerly called “'''Katte Myōjin'''.” {{ill|lt=Aonegamine|Q11661968|WD=Q11661968|1=Mount Aonegamine}}, which corresponds to the source of the {{ill|lt=Yoshino River (Nara Prefecture)|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshino River}}, has been revered since ancient times as a {{ill|lt=water deity|Q3137449|WD=Q3137449|1=Suijin}}. Accordingly, {{ill|lt=Kinpusen Shrine (Yoshino Town)|Q3197197|WD=Q3197197|lt=Kinpusen Shrine|1=Kinpu Shrine (Q3197197)}} was built near the summit (Oku-Senbon), {{ill|lt=Yoshino Mikumari Shrine|Q3529714|WD=Q3529714|1=Yoshino Mikumari Shrine}} on the mountainside (Kami-Senbon), and Katte Shrine at the mountain’s foot (Naka-Senbon)“The History of Popular Salvation and Buddhism (Vol. 2)” by Munetoshi Nakaya, Ikuhōsha, 2012. “Katte” is also said to mean “entrance” or “lower side,” and from its written form it came to be worshipped as a deity of contests and as a {{ill|lt=war god|Q11637440|WD=Q11637440|lt=god of battle|1=Military god}}. During the period of {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the honji (original Buddhist identity) of Katte Daimyōjin was said to be {{ill|lt=Bishamonten|Q866315|WD=Q866315|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}, leading to even greater veneration by the warrior class. In addition, because it is located at the entrance to {{ill|lt=Mount Yoshino|Q1036938|WD=Q1036938|1=Mount Yoshino}}, it was also called Yamaguchi Shrine“Nara: A Grown-Up’s Walking Guide,” Seibido Publishing, 2010. The date of foundation is unknown, but the *Nichiyūji Keitōki* records it as the 6th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōan|Q312821|WD=Q312821|1=Emperor Kōan}} ({{ill|lt=386 BCE|Q228485|WD=Q228485|1=386 BC}}). It is said that when {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181|lt=Prince Ōama|1=Emperor Tenmu}} played the koto in the shrine hall, {{ill|lt=celestial maidens|Q111467505|WD=Q111467505|1=Celestial maiden}} descended and danced, waving their sleeves five times. The mountain behind the shrine came to be called “Sodefuri-yama” (Sleeve-Waving Mountain). This legend is also said to be the origin of the court dance known as “{{ill|lt=Gosechi no Mai|Q11372604|WD=Q11372604|1=Gosechi no Mai}}”. Within the precincts remains a dance mound said to mark the spot where {{ill|lt=Shizuka Gozen|Q3088217|WD=Q3088217|1=Shizuka Gozen}}, the wife of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Q310445|WD=Q310445|1=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}}, performed a dance when she was captured by pursuers. The {{ill|lt=main hall|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}}, built in the {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style with a {{ill|lt=hiwada-buki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} roof, measuring eight bays by two bays, was designated a prefectural {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}}. However, it was destroyed by suspected arson in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}). The sacred object has since been transferred to the facing {{ill|lt=Yoshimizu Shrine|Q3572667|WD=Q3572667|1=Yoshimizu Shrine}}, and donations are being solicited for the reconstruction of the main hall. === Relationship with Zao Gongen and Komori Myōjin === {{Multiple image | footer = Katte Myōjin (left) and Komori Myōjin (right) as depicted in the Yoshino Mandala | image1 = Katte-Myojin.jpg | image2 = Komori-Myojin.jpg | total_width = 350 }} {{ill|lt=Zao Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}} ({{ill|lt=Kinpusen-ji|Q1741668|WD=Q1741668|1=Kimpusen-ji}}), Komori Gongen (Yoshino Mikumari Shrine), and Katte Gongen (Katte Shrine) were enshrined together as the Three Gongen at {{ill|lt=Mount Mitoku’s Sanbutsu-ji|Q1053703|WD=Q1053703|lt=Sanbutsu-ji in Hōki Province|1=Hōki Province}}. Zao Gongen was enshrined at the inner sanctuary ({{ill|lt=Nageire-dō|Q19826408|WD=Q19826408|1=Nageire-dō}}), Komori Gongen at the Jizō Hall, and Katte Gongen at the Monju Hall{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2007-12-26| url=https://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/224955/02-1bunkazaiseirihyou.pdf | title=“Proposal for Assets Listed on the Tentative List of World Heritage Sites”: Cultural Properties Included in the Assets (5-1) Inventory Table | work=Official Tottori Prefecture Website | accessdate=2013-04-15}}. Katte Myōjin was also enshrined independently at shrines throughout the country and serves as the head shrine of 28 Katte Shrines nationwide{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com/pdf/syuisho.pdf | title=Prospectus for Support of Katte Shrine Reconstruction | work=Official Website of Yoshimizu Shrine | accessdate=2013-04-10|archive-url=https://www.web.archive.org/web/20060219194625/http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com:80/pdf/syuisho.pdf |archive-date=2006-02-19 |url-status=dead |url-status-date=2024-09-21}}. Komori Myōjin, the enshrined deity of Yoshino Mikumari Shrine, is said to be the consort deity of Katte Myōjin, with Katte Myōjin as the male deity and Komori Myōjin as the female deity. In the Noh play “{{ill|lt=Arashiyama (Noh)|Q17988500|WD=Q17988500|lt=Arashiyama|1=Arashiyama}},” which was established in the late Muromachi period, an elderly couple who serve as guardians of the cherry blossoms of {{ill|lt=Arashiyama|Q2859566|WD=Q2859566|1=Arashiyama}}—transplanted from Yoshino—are revealed to be manifestations of the deities Katte and Komori. The play’s plot proclaims that Zao Gongen, Katte Myōjin, and Komori Myōjin are three aspects of a single divine entity{{Cite web || url=http://www.tessen.org/dictionary/explain/arashiyama/ | title=Arashiyama | work=Tessenkai Noh Theatre Dictionary | accessdate=2013-04-09}}. {{-}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Katte-jinja (Yoshino, Nara)}} {{Osm box|n|2725147868}} * [https://www.kattejinja.com/ Katte Shrine Reconstruction Committee] {{Shinto}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Nara Prefecture}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Katte Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Katte Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:奈良県の神社]] [[Category:奈良県の旧村社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:吉野町の建築物]] [[Category:吉野町の歴史]] [[Category:吉野・大峯]] [[Category:pages]]

in Yoshino Town, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Nara City|Katte Shrine (Nara City)}} {{Shrine | Image= [[File:Katte-jinja Yoshino Nara01n4272.jpg|250px]]
Torii gate | Name=Katte Shrine | Location={{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yoshino District|Q1154558|WD=Q1154558|1=Yoshino District, Nara}}{{ill|lt=Yoshino Town|Q1203731|WD=Q1203731|1=Yoshino, Nara}} Yoshinoyama 2354 | Coordinates={{Coord|34|21|52.9|N|135|51|44.9|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deity={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} | Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (major) disputed shrine
Former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | Founded=Traditionally: Year 6 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōan|Q312821|WD=Q312821|1=Emperor Kōan}} ({{ill|lt=386 BCE|Q228485|WD=Q228485|1=386 BC}}) | Main hall= | Annual festival=Third Sunday of October | Map = {{Location map|Japan Nara|label=Katte Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} '''Katte Shrine''' (かつてじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Yoshino District|Q1154558|WD=Q1154558|1=Yoshino District, Nara}}, {{ill|lt=Yoshino Town|Q1203731|WD=Q1203731|1=Yoshino, Nara}}. According to the *{{ill|lt=Wakan Sansai Zue|Q1135803|WD=Q1135803|1=Wakan Sansai Zue}}*, the enshrined deity is described as “Katte-sha: one enshrined deity, Aiman-no-Mikoto{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2569751 | title=Wakan Sansai Zue: 105 volumes, front 1 volume, end 1 volume [55] | work=National Diet Library Digital Collection | accessdate=2018-10-27}} (Ukeri-no-Mikoto / Ukenori-no-Mikoto).” It is also written as “Ukeman-no-Mikoto”{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=668 | title=Shrine Introduction > Shrines of Shiga Prefecture | work=Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines | accessdate=2018-10-27}}. The current principal enshrined deity is said to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōyama-tsumi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Kukunochi|Q10749920|WD=Q10749920|lt=Kukunochi-no-Mikoto|1=Kukunochi}}, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Koke-mushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hano-hime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} are also enshrined together. === Overview === It is one of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshino Hassha Myōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}, guardian shrines of {{ill|lt=Mount Ōmine|Q3695989|WD=Q3695989|1=Mount Ōmine}} in Yoshino, and was formerly called “'''Katte Myōjin'''.” {{ill|lt=Aonegamine|Q11661968|WD=Q11661968|1=Mount Aonegamine}}, which corresponds to the source of the {{ill|lt=Yoshino River (Nara Prefecture)|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshino River}}, has been revered since ancient times as a {{ill|lt=water deity|Q3137449|WD=Q3137449|1=Suijin}}. Accordingly, {{ill|lt=Kinpusen Shrine (Yoshino Town)|Q3197197|WD=Q3197197|lt=Kinpusen Shrine|1=Kinpu Shrine (Q3197197)}} was built near the summit (Oku-Senbon), {{ill|lt=Yoshino Mikumari Shrine|Q3529714|WD=Q3529714|1=Yoshino Mikumari Shrine}} on the mountainside (Kami-Senbon), and Katte Shrine at the mountain’s foot (Naka-Senbon)“The History of Popular Salvation and Buddhism (Vol. 2)” by Munetoshi Nakaya, Ikuhōsha, 2012. “Katte” is also said to mean “entrance” or “lower side,” and from its written form it came to be worshipped as a deity of contests and as a {{ill|lt=war god|Q11637440|WD=Q11637440|lt=god of battle|1=Military god}}. During the period of {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the honji (original Buddhist identity) of Katte Daimyōjin was said to be {{ill|lt=Bishamonten|Q866315|WD=Q866315|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}, leading to even greater veneration by the warrior class. In addition, because it is located at the entrance to {{ill|lt=Mount Yoshino|Q1036938|WD=Q1036938|1=Mount Yoshino}}, it was also called Yamaguchi Shrine“Nara: A Grown-Up’s Walking Guide,” Seibido Publishing, 2010. The date of foundation is unknown, but the *Nichiyūji Keitōki* records it as the 6th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōan|Q312821|WD=Q312821|1=Emperor Kōan}} ({{ill|lt=386 BCE|Q228485|WD=Q228485|1=386 BC}}). It is said that when {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181|lt=Prince Ōama|1=Emperor Tenmu}} played the koto in the shrine hall, {{ill|lt=celestial maidens|Q111467505|WD=Q111467505|1=Celestial maiden}} descended and danced, waving their sleeves five times. The mountain behind the shrine came to be called “Sodefuri-yama” (Sleeve-Waving Mountain). This legend is also said to be the origin of the court dance known as “{{ill|lt=Gosechi no Mai|Q11372604|WD=Q11372604|1=Gosechi no Mai}}”. Within the precincts remains a dance mound said to mark the spot where {{ill|lt=Shizuka Gozen|Q3088217|WD=Q3088217|1=Shizuka Gozen}}, the wife of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Q310445|WD=Q310445|1=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}}, performed a dance when she was captured by pursuers. The {{ill|lt=main hall|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}}, built in the {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style with a {{ill|lt=hiwada-buki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} roof, measuring eight bays by two bays, was designated a prefectural {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}}. However, it was destroyed by suspected arson in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}). The sacred object has since been transferred to the facing {{ill|lt=Yoshimizu Shrine|Q3572667|WD=Q3572667|1=Yoshimizu Shrine}}, and donations are being solicited for the reconstruction of the main hall. === Relationship with Zao Gongen and Komori Myōjin === {{Multiple image | footer = Katte Myōjin (left) and Komori Myōjin (right) as depicted in the Yoshino Mandala | image1 = Katte-Myojin.jpg | image2 = Komori-Myojin.jpg | total_width = 350 }} {{ill|lt=Zao Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}} ({{ill|lt=Kinpusen-ji|Q1741668|WD=Q1741668|1=Kimpusen-ji}}), Komori Gongen (Yoshino Mikumari Shrine), and Katte Gongen (Katte Shrine) were enshrined together as the Three Gongen at {{ill|lt=Mount Mitoku’s Sanbutsu-ji|Q1053703|WD=Q1053703|lt=Sanbutsu-ji in Hōki Province|1=Hōki Province}}. Zao Gongen was enshrined at the inner sanctuary ({{ill|lt=Nageire-dō|Q19826408|WD=Q19826408|1=Nageire-dō}}), Komori Gongen at the Jizō Hall, and Katte Gongen at the Monju Hall{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2007-12-26| url=https://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/224955/02-1bunkazaiseirihyou.pdf | title=“Proposal for Assets Listed on the Tentative List of World Heritage Sites”: Cultural Properties Included in the Assets (5-1) Inventory Table | work=Official Tottori Prefecture Website | accessdate=2013-04-15}}. Katte Myōjin was also enshrined independently at shrines throughout the country and serves as the head shrine of 28 Katte Shrines nationwide{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com/pdf/syuisho.pdf | title=Prospectus for Support of Katte Shrine Reconstruction | work=Official Website of Yoshimizu Shrine | accessdate=2013-04-10|archive-url=https://www.web.archive.org/web/20060219194625/http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com:80/pdf/syuisho.pdf |archive-date=2006-02-19 |url-status=dead |url-status-date=2024-09-21}}. Komori Myōjin, the enshrined deity of Yoshino Mikumari Shrine, is said to be the consort deity of Katte Myōjin, with Katte Myōjin as the male deity and Komori Myōjin as the female deity. In the Noh play “{{ill|lt=Arashiyama (Noh)|Q17988500|WD=Q17988500|lt=Arashiyama|1=Arashiyama}},” which was established in the late Muromachi period, an elderly couple who serve as guardians of the cherry blossoms of {{ill|lt=Arashiyama|Q2859566|WD=Q2859566|1=Arashiyama}}—transplanted from Yoshino—are revealed to be manifestations of the deities Katte and Komori. The play’s plot proclaims that Zao Gongen, Katte Myōjin, and Komori Myōjin are three aspects of a single divine entity{{Cite web || url=http://www.tessen.org/dictionary/explain/arashiyama/ | title=Arashiyama | work=Tessenkai Noh Theatre Dictionary | accessdate=2013-04-09}}. {{-}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Katte-jinja (Yoshino, Nara)}} {{Osm box|n|2725147868}} * [https://www.kattejinja.com/ Katte Shrine Reconstruction Committee] {{Shinto}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Nara Prefecture}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Katte Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Katte Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:奈良県の神社]] [[Category:Son-sha in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:吉野町の建築物]] [[Category:吉野町の歴史]] [[Category:吉野・大峯]] [[Category:pages]]

According to the *{{ill|lt=Wakan Sansai Zue|Q1135803|WD=Q1135803|1=Wakan Sansai Zue}}*, the enshrined deity is described as “Katte-sha: one enshrined deity, Aiman-no-Mikoto{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2569751 | title=Wakan Sansai Zue: 105 volumes, front 1 volume, end 1 volume [55] | work=National Diet Library Digital Collection | accessdate=2018-10-27}} (Ukeri-no-Mikoto / Ukenori-no-Mikoto).” It is also written as “Ukeman-no-Mikoto”{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=668 | title=Shrine Introduction > Shrines of Shiga Prefecture | work=Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines | accessdate=2018-10-27}}. The current principal enshrined deity is said to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōyama-tsumi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Kukunochi|Q10749920|WD=Q10749920|lt=Kukunochi-no-Mikoto|1=Kukunochi}}, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Koke-mushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hano-hime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} are also enshrined together. === Overview === It is one of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshino Hassha Myōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}, guardian shrines of {{ill|lt=Mount Ōmine|Q3695989|WD=Q3695989|1=Mount Ōmine}} in Yoshino, and was formerly called “'''Katte Myōjin'''.” {{ill|lt=Aonegamine|Q11661968|WD=Q11661968|1=Mount Aonegamine}}, which corresponds to the source of the {{ill|lt=Yoshino River (Nara Prefecture)|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshino River}}, has been revered since ancient times as a {{ill|lt=water deity|Q3137449|WD=Q3137449|1=Suijin}}. Accordingly, {{ill|lt=Kinpusen Shrine (Yoshino Town)|Q3197197|WD=Q3197197|lt=Kinpusen Shrine|1=Kinpu Shrine (Q3197197)}} was built near the summit (Oku-Senbon), {{ill|lt=Yoshino Mikumari Shrine|Q3529714|WD=Q3529714|1=Yoshino Mikumari Shrine}} on the mountainside (Kami-Senbon), and Katte Shrine at the mountain’s foot (Naka-Senbon)“The History of Popular Salvation and Buddhism (Vol. 2)” by Munetoshi Nakaya, Ikuhōsha, 2012. “Katte” is also said to mean “entrance” or “lower side,” and from its written form it came to be worshipped as a deity of contests and as a {{ill|lt=war god|Q11637440|WD=Q11637440|lt=god of battle|1=Military god}}. During the period of {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the honji (original Buddhist identity) of Katte Daimyōjin was said to be {{ill|lt=Bishamonten|Q866315|WD=Q866315|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}, leading to even greater veneration by the warrior class. In addition, because it is located at the entrance to {{ill|lt=Mount Yoshino|Q1036938|WD=Q1036938|1=Mount Yoshino}}, it was also called Yamaguchi Shrine“Nara: A Grown-Up’s Walking Guide,” Seibido Publishing, 2010. The date of foundation is unknown, but the *Nichiyūji Keitōki* records it as the 6th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōan|Q312821|WD=Q312821|1=Emperor Kōan}} ({{ill|lt=386 BCE|Q228485|WD=Q228485|1=386 BC}}). It is said that when {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181|lt=Prince Ōama|1=Emperor Tenmu}} played the koto in the shrine hall, {{ill|lt=celestial maidens|Q111467505|WD=Q111467505|1=Celestial maiden}} descended and danced, waving their sleeves five times. The mountain behind the shrine came to be called “Sodefuri-yama” (Sleeve-Waving Mountain). This legend is also said to be the origin of the court dance known as “{{ill|lt=Gosechi no Mai|Q11372604|WD=Q11372604|1=Gosechi no Mai}}”. Within the precincts remains a dance mound said to mark the spot where {{ill|lt=Shizuka Gozen|Q3088217|WD=Q3088217|1=Shizuka Gozen}}, the wife of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Q310445|WD=Q310445|1=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}}, performed a dance when she was captured by pursuers. The {{ill|lt=main hall|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}}, built in the {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style with a {{ill|lt=hiwada-buki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} roof, measuring eight bays by two bays, was designated a prefectural {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}}. However, it was destroyed by suspected arson in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}). The sacred object has since been transferred to the facing {{ill|lt=Yoshimizu Shrine|Q3572667|WD=Q3572667|1=Yoshimizu Shrine}}, and donations are being solicited for the reconstruction of the main hall. === Relationship with Zao Gongen and Komori Myōjin === {{Multiple image | footer = Katte Myōjin (left) and Komori Myōjin (right) as depicted in the Yoshino Mandala | image1 = Katte-Myojin.jpg | image2 = Komori-Myojin.jpg | total_width = 350 }} {{ill|lt=Zao Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}} ({{ill|lt=Kinpusen-ji|Q1741668|WD=Q1741668|1=Kimpusen-ji}}), Komori Gongen (Yoshino Mikumari Shrine), and Katte Gongen (Katte Shrine) were enshrined together as the Three Gongen at {{ill|lt=Mount Mitoku’s Sanbutsu-ji|Q1053703|WD=Q1053703|lt=Sanbutsu-ji in Hōki Province|1=Hōki Province}}. Zao Gongen was enshrined at the inner sanctuary ({{ill|lt=Nageire-dō|Q19826408|WD=Q19826408|1=Nageire-dō}}), Komori Gongen at the Jizō Hall, and Katte Gongen at the Monju Hall{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2007-12-26| url=https://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/224955/02-1bunkazaiseirihyou.pdf | title=“Proposal for Assets Listed on the Tentative List of World Heritage Sites”: Cultural Properties Included in the Assets (5-1) Inventory Table | work=Official Tottori Prefecture Website | accessdate=2013-04-15}}. Katte Myōjin was also enshrined independently at shrines throughout the country and serves as the head shrine of 28 Katte Shrines nationwide{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com/pdf/syuisho.pdf | title=Prospectus for Support of Katte Shrine Reconstruction | work=Official Website of Yoshimizu Shrine | accessdate=2013-04-10|archive-url=https://www.web.archive.org/web/20060219194625/http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com:80/pdf/syuisho.pdf |archive-date=2006-02-19 |url-status=dead |url-status-date=2024-09-21}}. Komori Myōjin, the enshrined deity of Yoshino Mikumari Shrine, is said to be the consort deity of Katte Myōjin, with Katte Myōjin as the male deity and Komori Myōjin as the female deity. In the Noh play “{{ill|lt=Arashiyama (Noh)|Q17988500|WD=Q17988500|lt=Arashiyama|1=Arashiyama}},” which was established in the late Muromachi period, an elderly couple who serve as guardians of the cherry blossoms of {{ill|lt=Arashiyama|Q2859566|WD=Q2859566|1=Arashiyama}}—transplanted from Yoshino—are revealed to be manifestations of the deities Katte and Komori. The play’s plot proclaims that Zao Gongen, Katte Myōjin, and Komori Myōjin are three aspects of a single divine entity{{Cite web || url=http://www.tessen.org/dictionary/explain/arashiyama/ | title=Arashiyama | work=Tessenkai Noh Theatre Dictionary | accessdate=2013-04-09}}. {{-}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Katte-jinja (Yoshino, Nara)}} {{Osm box|n|2725147868}} * [https://www.kattejinja.com/ Katte Shrine Reconstruction Committee] {{Shinto}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Nara Prefecture}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Katte Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Katte Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:吉野町の建築物]] [[Category:吉野町の歴史]] [[Category:吉野・大峯]] [[Category:pages]]

Torii gate | Name=Katte Shrine | Location={{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yoshino District|Q1154558|WD=Q1154558|1=Yoshino District, Nara}}{{ill|lt=Yoshino Town|Q1203731|WD=Q1203731|1=Yoshino, Nara}} Yoshinoyama 2354 | Coordinates={{Coord|34|21|52.9|N|135|51|44.9|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deity={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} | Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (major) disputed shrine
Former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | Founded=Traditionally: Year 6 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōan|Q312821|WD=Q312821|1=Emperor Kōan}} ({{ill|lt=386 BCE|Q228485|WD=Q228485|1=386 BC}}) | Main hall= | Annual festival=Third Sunday of October | Map = {{Location map|Japan Nara|label=Katte Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} '''Katte Shrine''' (かつてじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Yoshino District|Q1154558|WD=Q1154558|1=Yoshino District, Nara}}, {{ill|lt=Yoshino Town|Q1203731|WD=Q1203731|1=Yoshino, Nara}}. According to the *{{ill|lt=Wakan Sansai Zue|Q1135803|WD=Q1135803|1=Wakan Sansai Zue}}*, the enshrined deity is described as “Katte-sha: one enshrined deity, Aiman-no-Mikoto{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2569751 | title=Wakan Sansai Zue: 105 volumes, front 1 volume, end 1 volume [55] | work=National Diet Library Digital Collection | accessdate=2018-10-27}} (Ukeri-no-Mikoto / Ukenori-no-Mikoto).” It is also written as “Ukeman-no-Mikoto”{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=668 | title=Shrine Introduction > Shrines of Shiga Prefecture | work=Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines | accessdate=2018-10-27}}. The current principal enshrined deity is said to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōyama-tsumi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Kukunochi|Q10749920|WD=Q10749920|lt=Kukunochi-no-Mikoto|1=Kukunochi}}, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Koke-mushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hano-hime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} are also enshrined together. === Overview === It is one of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshino Hassha Myōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}, guardian shrines of {{ill|lt=Mount Ōmine|Q3695989|WD=Q3695989|1=Mount Ōmine}} in Yoshino, and was formerly called “'''Katte Myōjin'''.” {{ill|lt=Aonegamine|Q11661968|WD=Q11661968|1=Mount Aonegamine}}, which corresponds to the source of the {{ill|lt=Yoshino River (Nara Prefecture)|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshino River}}, has been revered since ancient times as a {{ill|lt=water deity|Q3137449|WD=Q3137449|1=Suijin}}. Accordingly, {{ill|lt=Kinpusen Shrine (Yoshino Town)|Q3197197|WD=Q3197197|lt=Kinpusen Shrine|1=Kinpu Shrine (Q3197197)}} was built near the summit (Oku-Senbon), {{ill|lt=Yoshino Mikumari Shrine|Q3529714|WD=Q3529714|1=Yoshino Mikumari Shrine}} on the mountainside (Kami-Senbon), and Katte Shrine at the mountain’s foot (Naka-Senbon)“The History of Popular Salvation and Buddhism (Vol. 2)” by Munetoshi Nakaya, Ikuhōsha, 2012. “Katte” is also said to mean “entrance” or “lower side,” and from its written form it came to be worshipped as a deity of contests and as a {{ill|lt=war god|Q11637440|WD=Q11637440|lt=god of battle|1=Military god}}. During the period of {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the honji (original Buddhist identity) of Katte Daimyōjin was said to be {{ill|lt=Bishamonten|Q866315|WD=Q866315|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}, leading to even greater veneration by the warrior class. In addition, because it is located at the entrance to {{ill|lt=Mount Yoshino|Q1036938|WD=Q1036938|1=Mount Yoshino}}, it was also called Yamaguchi Shrine“Nara: A Grown-Up’s Walking Guide,” Seibido Publishing, 2010. The date of foundation is unknown, but the *Nichiyūji Keitōki* records it as the 6th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōan|Q312821|WD=Q312821|1=Emperor Kōan}} ({{ill|lt=386 BCE|Q228485|WD=Q228485|1=386 BC}}). It is said that when {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181|lt=Prince Ōama|1=Emperor Tenmu}} played the koto in the shrine hall, {{ill|lt=celestial maidens|Q111467505|WD=Q111467505|1=Celestial maiden}} descended and danced, waving their sleeves five times. The mountain behind the shrine came to be called “Sodefuri-yama” (Sleeve-Waving Mountain). This legend is also said to be the origin of the court dance known as “{{ill|lt=Gosechi no Mai|Q11372604|WD=Q11372604|1=Gosechi no Mai}}”. Within the precincts remains a dance mound said to mark the spot where {{ill|lt=Shizuka Gozen|Q3088217|WD=Q3088217|1=Shizuka Gozen}}, the wife of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Q310445|WD=Q310445|1=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}}, performed a dance when she was captured by pursuers. The {{ill|lt=main hall|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}}, built in the {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style with a {{ill|lt=hiwada-buki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} roof, measuring eight bays by two bays, was designated a prefectural {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}}. However, it was destroyed by suspected arson in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}). The sacred object has since been transferred to the facing {{ill|lt=Yoshimizu Shrine|Q3572667|WD=Q3572667|1=Yoshimizu Shrine}}, and donations are being solicited for the reconstruction of the main hall. === Relationship with Zao Gongen and Komori Myōjin === {{Multiple image | footer = Katte Myōjin (left) and Komori Myōjin (right) as depicted in the Yoshino Mandala | image1 = Katte-Myojin.jpg | image2 = Komori-Myojin.jpg | total_width = 350 }} {{ill|lt=Zao Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}} ({{ill|lt=Kinpusen-ji|Q1741668|WD=Q1741668|1=Kimpusen-ji}}), Komori Gongen (Yoshino Mikumari Shrine), and Katte Gongen (Katte Shrine) were enshrined together as the Three Gongen at {{ill|lt=Mount Mitoku’s Sanbutsu-ji|Q1053703|WD=Q1053703|lt=Sanbutsu-ji in Hōki Province|1=Hōki Province}}. Zao Gongen was enshrined at the inner sanctuary ({{ill|lt=Nageire-dō|Q19826408|WD=Q19826408|1=Nageire-dō}}), Komori Gongen at the Jizō Hall, and Katte Gongen at the Monju Hall{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2007-12-26| url=https://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/224955/02-1bunkazaiseirihyou.pdf | title=“Proposal for Assets Listed on the Tentative List of World Heritage Sites”: Cultural Properties Included in the Assets (5-1) Inventory Table | work=Official Tottori Prefecture Website | accessdate=2013-04-15}}. Katte Myōjin was also enshrined independently at shrines throughout the country and serves as the head shrine of 28 Katte Shrines nationwide{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com/pdf/syuisho.pdf | title=Prospectus for Support of Katte Shrine Reconstruction | work=Official Website of Yoshimizu Shrine | accessdate=2013-04-10|archive-url=https://www.web.archive.org/web/20060219194625/http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com:80/pdf/syuisho.pdf |archive-date=2006-02-19 |url-status=dead |url-status-date=2024-09-21}}. Komori Myōjin, the enshrined deity of Yoshino Mikumari Shrine, is said to be the consort deity of Katte Myōjin, with Katte Myōjin as the male deity and Komori Myōjin as the female deity. In the Noh play “{{ill|lt=Arashiyama (Noh)|Q17988500|WD=Q17988500|lt=Arashiyama|1=Arashiyama}},” which was established in the late Muromachi period, an elderly couple who serve as guardians of the cherry blossoms of {{ill|lt=Arashiyama|Q2859566|WD=Q2859566|1=Arashiyama}}—transplanted from Yoshino—are revealed to be manifestations of the deities Katte and Komori. The play’s plot proclaims that Zao Gongen, Katte Myōjin, and Komori Myōjin are three aspects of a single divine entity{{Cite web || url=http://www.tessen.org/dictionary/explain/arashiyama/ | title=Arashiyama | work=Tessenkai Noh Theatre Dictionary | accessdate=2013-04-09}}. {{-}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Katte-jinja (Yoshino, Nara)}} {{Osm box|n|2725147868}} * [https://www.kattejinja.com/ Katte Shrine Reconstruction Committee] {{Shinto}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Nara Prefecture}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Katte Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Katte Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:吉野町の建築物]] [[Category:吉野町の歴史]] [[Category:吉野・大峯]]

in Yoshino Town, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Nara City|Katte Shrine (Nara City)}} {{Shrine | Image= [[File:Katte-jinja Yoshino Nara01n4272.jpg|250px]]
Torii gate | Name=Katte Shrine | Location={{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yoshino District|Q1154558|WD=Q1154558|1=Yoshino District, Nara}}{{ill|lt=Yoshino Town|Q1203731|WD=Q1203731|1=Yoshino, Nara}} Yoshinoyama 2354 | Coordinates={{Coord|34|21|52.9|N|135|51|44.9|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deity={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} | Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (major) disputed shrine
Former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | Founded=Traditionally: Year 6 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōan|Q312821|WD=Q312821|1=Emperor Kōan}} ({{ill|lt=386 BCE|Q228485|WD=Q228485|1=386 BC}}) | Main hall= | Annual festival=Third Sunday of October | Map = {{Location map|Japan Nara|label=Katte Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} '''Katte Shrine''' (かつてじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Yoshino District|Q1154558|WD=Q1154558|1=Yoshino District, Nara}}, {{ill|lt=Yoshino Town|Q1203731|WD=Q1203731|1=Yoshino, Nara}}. According to the *{{ill|lt=Wakan Sansai Zue|Q1135803|WD=Q1135803|1=Wakan Sansai Zue}}*, the enshrined deity is described as “Katte-sha: one enshrined deity, Aiman-no-Mikoto{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2569751 | title=Wakan Sansai Zue: 105 volumes, front 1 volume, end 1 volume [55] | work=National Diet Library Digital Collection | accessdate=2018-10-27}} (Ukeri-no-Mikoto / Ukenori-no-Mikoto).” It is also written as “Ukeman-no-Mikoto”{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=668 | title=Shrine Introduction > Shrines of Shiga Prefecture | work=Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines | accessdate=2018-10-27}}. The current principal enshrined deity is said to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōyama-tsumi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Kukunochi|Q10749920|WD=Q10749920|lt=Kukunochi-no-Mikoto|1=Kukunochi}}, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Koke-mushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hano-hime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} are also enshrined together. === Overview === It is one of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshino Hassha Myōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}, guardian shrines of {{ill|lt=Mount Ōmine|Q3695989|WD=Q3695989|1=Mount Ōmine}} in Yoshino, and was formerly called “'''Katte Myōjin'''.” {{ill|lt=Aonegamine|Q11661968|WD=Q11661968|1=Mount Aonegamine}}, which corresponds to the source of the {{ill|lt=Yoshino River (Nara Prefecture)|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshino River}}, has been revered since ancient times as a {{ill|lt=water deity|Q3137449|WD=Q3137449|1=Suijin}}. Accordingly, {{ill|lt=Kinpusen Shrine (Yoshino Town)|Q3197197|WD=Q3197197|lt=Kinpusen Shrine|1=Kinpu Shrine (Q3197197)}} was built near the summit (Oku-Senbon), {{ill|lt=Yoshino Mikumari Shrine|Q3529714|WD=Q3529714|1=Yoshino Mikumari Shrine}} on the mountainside (Kami-Senbon), and Katte Shrine at the mountain’s foot (Naka-Senbon)“The History of Popular Salvation and Buddhism (Vol. 2)” by Munetoshi Nakaya, Ikuhōsha, 2012. “Katte” is also said to mean “entrance” or “lower side,” and from its written form it came to be worshipped as a deity of contests and as a {{ill|lt=war god|Q11637440|WD=Q11637440|lt=god of battle|1=Military god}}. During the period of {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the honji (original Buddhist identity) of Katte Daimyōjin was said to be {{ill|lt=Bishamonten|Q866315|WD=Q866315|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}, leading to even greater veneration by the warrior class. In addition, because it is located at the entrance to {{ill|lt=Mount Yoshino|Q1036938|WD=Q1036938|1=Mount Yoshino}}, it was also called Yamaguchi Shrine“Nara: A Grown-Up’s Walking Guide,” Seibido Publishing, 2010. The date of foundation is unknown, but the *Nichiyūji Keitōki* records it as the 6th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōan|Q312821|WD=Q312821|1=Emperor Kōan}} ({{ill|lt=386 BCE|Q228485|WD=Q228485|1=386 BC}}). It is said that when {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181|lt=Prince Ōama|1=Emperor Tenmu}} played the koto in the shrine hall, {{ill|lt=celestial maidens|Q111467505|WD=Q111467505|1=Celestial maiden}} descended and danced, waving their sleeves five times. The mountain behind the shrine came to be called “Sodefuri-yama” (Sleeve-Waving Mountain). This legend is also said to be the origin of the court dance known as “{{ill|lt=Gosechi no Mai|Q11372604|WD=Q11372604|1=Gosechi no Mai}}”. Within the precincts remains a dance mound said to mark the spot where {{ill|lt=Shizuka Gozen|Q3088217|WD=Q3088217|1=Shizuka Gozen}}, the wife of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Q310445|WD=Q310445|1=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}}, performed a dance when she was captured by pursuers. The {{ill|lt=main hall|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}}, built in the {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style with a {{ill|lt=hiwada-buki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} roof, measuring eight bays by two bays, was designated a prefectural {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}}. However, it was destroyed by suspected arson in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}). The sacred object has since been transferred to the facing {{ill|lt=Yoshimizu Shrine|Q3572667|WD=Q3572667|1=Yoshimizu Shrine}}, and donations are being solicited for the reconstruction of the main hall. === Relationship with Zao Gongen and Komori Myōjin === {{Multiple image | footer = Katte Myōjin (left) and Komori Myōjin (right) as depicted in the Yoshino Mandala | image1 = Katte-Myojin.jpg | image2 = Komori-Myojin.jpg | total_width = 350 }} {{ill|lt=Zao Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}} ({{ill|lt=Kinpusen-ji|Q1741668|WD=Q1741668|1=Kimpusen-ji}}), Komori Gongen (Yoshino Mikumari Shrine), and Katte Gongen (Katte Shrine) were enshrined together as the Three Gongen at {{ill|lt=Mount Mitoku’s Sanbutsu-ji|Q1053703|WD=Q1053703|lt=Sanbutsu-ji in Hōki Province|1=Hōki Province}}. Zao Gongen was enshrined at the inner sanctuary ({{ill|lt=Nageire-dō|Q19826408|WD=Q19826408|1=Nageire-dō}}), Komori Gongen at the Jizō Hall, and Katte Gongen at the Monju Hall{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2007-12-26| url=https://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/224955/02-1bunkazaiseirihyou.pdf | title=“Proposal for Assets Listed on the Tentative List of World Heritage Sites”: Cultural Properties Included in the Assets (5-1) Inventory Table | work=Official Tottori Prefecture Website | accessdate=2013-04-15}}. Katte Myōjin was also enshrined independently at shrines throughout the country and serves as the head shrine of 28 Katte Shrines nationwide{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com/pdf/syuisho.pdf | title=Prospectus for Support of Katte Shrine Reconstruction | work=Official Website of Yoshimizu Shrine | accessdate=2013-04-10|archive-url=https://www.web.archive.org/web/20060219194625/http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com:80/pdf/syuisho.pdf |archive-date=2006-02-19 |url-status=dead |url-status-date=2024-09-21}}. Komori Myōjin, the enshrined deity of Yoshino Mikumari Shrine, is said to be the consort deity of Katte Myōjin, with Katte Myōjin as the male deity and Komori Myōjin as the female deity. In the Noh play “{{ill|lt=Arashiyama (Noh)|Q17988500|WD=Q17988500|lt=Arashiyama|1=Arashiyama}},” which was established in the late Muromachi period, an elderly couple who serve as guardians of the cherry blossoms of {{ill|lt=Arashiyama|Q2859566|WD=Q2859566|1=Arashiyama}}—transplanted from Yoshino—are revealed to be manifestations of the deities Katte and Komori. The play’s plot proclaims that Zao Gongen, Katte Myōjin, and Komori Myōjin are three aspects of a single divine entity{{Cite web || url=http://www.tessen.org/dictionary/explain/arashiyama/ | title=Arashiyama | work=Tessenkai Noh Theatre Dictionary | accessdate=2013-04-09}}. {{-}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Katte-jinja (Yoshino, Nara)}} {{Osm box|n|2725147868}} * [https://www.kattejinja.com/ Katte Shrine Reconstruction Committee] {{Shinto}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Nara Prefecture}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Katte Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Katte Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:吉野町の建築物]] [[Category:吉野町の歴史]] [[Category:Yoshino and Ōmine]]

'奈良県吉野郡吉野町吉野山2354', 'language': 'ja'} == Japan Search name ID (P6698) == * 勝手神社 == located in/on physical feature (P706) == * {{ill|Q1036938|ltq=Q1036938|WD=Q1036938|1=Mount Yoshino}} == image of entrance (P9721) == * Katte-jinja Yoshino Nara01n4272.jpg {{commons category|Category:Katsute-jinja (Yoshino, Nara)}} [[JA:勝手神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11400330}} == Japanese content == {{otheruses|Shrine in Yoshino Town, Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Nara City|Katte Shrine (Nara City)}} {{Shrine | Image= [[File:Katte-jinja Yoshino Nara01n4272.jpg|250px]]
Torii gate | Name=Katte Shrine | Location={{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yoshino District|Q1154558|WD=Q1154558|1=Yoshino District, Nara}}{{ill|lt=Yoshino Town|Q1203731|WD=Q1203731|1=Yoshino, Nara}} Yoshinoyama 2354 | Coordinates={{Coord|34|21|52.9|N|135|51|44.9|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Enshrined deity={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} | Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (major) disputed shrine
Former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | Founded=Traditionally: Year 6 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōan|Q312821|WD=Q312821|1=Emperor Kōan}} ({{ill|lt=386 BCE|Q228485|WD=Q228485|1=386 BC}}) | Main hall= | Annual festival=Third Sunday of October | Map = {{Location map|Japan Nara|label=Katte Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} '''Katte Shrine''' (かつてじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Yoshino District|Q1154558|WD=Q1154558|1=Yoshino District, Nara}}, {{ill|lt=Yoshino Town|Q1203731|WD=Q1203731|1=Yoshino, Nara}}. According to the *{{ill|lt=Wakan Sansai Zue|Q1135803|WD=Q1135803|1=Wakan Sansai Zue}}*, the enshrined deity is described as “Katte-sha: one enshrined deity, Aiman-no-Mikoto{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2569751 | title=Wakan Sansai Zue: 105 volumes, front 1 volume, end 1 volume [55] | work=National Diet Library Digital Collection | accessdate=2018-10-27}} (Ukeri-no-Mikoto / Ukenori-no-Mikoto).” It is also written as “Ukeman-no-Mikoto”{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=http://www.shiga-jinjacho.jp/ycBBS/Board.cgi/02_jinja_db/db/ycDB_02jinja-pc-detail.html?mode:view=1&view:oid=668 | title=Shrine Introduction > Shrines of Shiga Prefecture | work=Shiga Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines | accessdate=2018-10-27}}. The current principal enshrined deity is said to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōyama-tsumi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Kukunochi|Q10749920|WD=Q10749920|lt=Kukunochi-no-Mikoto|1=Kukunochi}}, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Koke-mushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hano-hime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} are also enshrined together. === Overview === It is one of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshino Hassha Myōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}, guardian shrines of {{ill|lt=Mount Ōmine|Q3695989|WD=Q3695989|1=Mount Ōmine}} in Yoshino, and was formerly called “'''Katte Myōjin'''.” {{ill|lt=Aonegamine|Q11661968|WD=Q11661968|1=Mount Aonegamine}}, which corresponds to the source of the {{ill|lt=Yoshino River (Nara Prefecture)|UNKNOWN|lt=Yoshino River}}, has been revered since ancient times as a {{ill|lt=water deity|Q3137449|WD=Q3137449|1=Suijin}}. Accordingly, {{ill|lt=Kinpusen Shrine (Yoshino Town)|Q3197197|WD=Q3197197|lt=Kinpusen Shrine|1=Kinpu Shrine (Q3197197)}} was built near the summit (Oku-Senbon), {{ill|lt=Yoshino Mikumari Shrine|Q3529714|WD=Q3529714|1=Yoshino Mikumari Shrine}} on the mountainside (Kami-Senbon), and Katte Shrine at the mountain’s foot (Naka-Senbon)“The History of Popular Salvation and Buddhism (Vol. 2)” by Munetoshi Nakaya, Ikuhōsha, 2012. “Katte” is also said to mean “entrance” or “lower side,” and from its written form it came to be worshipped as a deity of contests and as a {{ill|lt=war god|Q11637440|WD=Q11637440|lt=god of battle|1=Military god}}. During the period of {{ill|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism), the honji (original Buddhist identity) of Katte Daimyōjin was said to be {{ill|lt=Bishamonten|Q866315|WD=Q866315|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}, leading to even greater veneration by the warrior class. In addition, because it is located at the entrance to {{ill|lt=Mount Yoshino|Q1036938|WD=Q1036938|1=Mount Yoshino}}, it was also called Yamaguchi Shrine“Nara: A Grown-Up’s Walking Guide,” Seibido Publishing, 2010. The date of foundation is unknown, but the *Nichiyūji Keitōki* records it as the 6th year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Kōan|Q312821|WD=Q312821|1=Emperor Kōan}} ({{ill|lt=386 BCE|Q228485|WD=Q228485|1=386 BC}}). It is said that when {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181|lt=Prince Ōama|1=Emperor Tenmu}} played the koto in the shrine hall, {{ill|lt=celestial maidens|Q111467505|WD=Q111467505|1=Celestial maiden}} descended and danced, waving their sleeves five times. The mountain behind the shrine came to be called “Sodefuri-yama” (Sleeve-Waving Mountain). This legend is also said to be the origin of the court dance known as “{{ill|lt=Gosechi no Mai|Q11372604|WD=Q11372604|1=Gosechi no Mai}}”. Within the precincts remains a dance mound said to mark the spot where {{ill|lt=Shizuka Gozen|Q3088217|WD=Q3088217|1=Shizuka Gozen}}, the wife of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Q310445|WD=Q310445|1=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}}, performed a dance when she was captured by pursuers. The {{ill|lt=main hall|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}}, built in the {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style with a {{ill|lt=hiwada-buki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} roof, measuring eight bays by two bays, was designated a prefectural {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}}. However, it was destroyed by suspected arson in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}). The sacred object has since been transferred to the facing {{ill|lt=Yoshimizu Shrine|Q3572667|WD=Q3572667|1=Yoshimizu Shrine}}, and donations are being solicited for the reconstruction of the main hall. === Relationship with Zao Gongen and Komori Myōjin === {{Multiple image | footer = Katte Myōjin (left) and Komori Myōjin (right) as depicted in the Yoshino Mandala | image1 = Katte-Myojin.jpg | image2 = Komori-Myojin.jpg | total_width = 350 }} {{ill|lt=Zao Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}} ({{ill|lt=Kinpusen-ji|Q1741668|WD=Q1741668|1=Kimpusen-ji}}), Komori Gongen (Yoshino Mikumari Shrine), and Katte Gongen (Katte Shrine) were enshrined together as the Three Gongen at {{ill|lt=Mount Mitoku’s Sanbutsu-ji|Q1053703|WD=Q1053703|lt=Sanbutsu-ji in Hōki Province|1=Hōki Province}}. Zao Gongen was enshrined at the inner sanctuary ({{ill|lt=Nageire-dō|Q19826408|WD=Q19826408|1=Nageire-dō}}), Komori Gongen at the Jizō Hall, and Katte Gongen at the Monju Hall{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2007-12-26| url=https://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/224955/02-1bunkazaiseirihyou.pdf | title=“Proposal for Assets Listed on the Tentative List of World Heritage Sites”: Cultural Properties Included in the Assets (5-1) Inventory Table | work=Official Tottori Prefecture Website | accessdate=2013-04-15}}. Katte Myōjin was also enshrined independently at shrines throughout the country and serves as the head shrine of 28 Katte Shrines nationwide{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com/pdf/syuisho.pdf | title=Prospectus for Support of Katte Shrine Reconstruction | work=Official Website of Yoshimizu Shrine | accessdate=2013-04-10|archive-url=https://www.web.archive.org/web/20060219194625/http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com:80/pdf/syuisho.pdf |archive-date=2006-02-19 |url-status=dead |url-status-date=2024-09-21}}. Komori Myōjin, the enshrined deity of Yoshino Mikumari Shrine, is said to be the consort deity of Katte Myōjin, with Katte Myōjin as the male deity and Komori Myōjin as the female deity. In the Noh play “{{ill|lt=Arashiyama (Noh)|Q17988500|WD=Q17988500|lt=Arashiyama|1=Arashiyama}},” which was established in the late Muromachi period, an elderly couple who serve as guardians of the cherry blossoms of {{ill|lt=Arashiyama|Q2859566|WD=Q2859566|1=Arashiyama}}—transplanted from Yoshino—are revealed to be manifestations of the deities Katte and Komori. The play’s plot proclaims that Zao Gongen, Katte Myōjin, and Komori Myōjin are three aspects of a single divine entity{{Cite web || url=http://www.tessen.org/dictionary/explain/arashiyama/ | title=Arashiyama | work=Tessenkai Noh Theatre Dictionary | accessdate=2013-04-09}}. {{-}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Katte-jinja (Yoshino, Nara)}} {{Osm box|n|2725147868}} * [https://www.kattejinja.com/ Katte Shrine Reconstruction Committee] {{Shinto}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Nara Prefecture}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Katte Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Katte Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Yoshino]] [[Category:吉野町の歴史]] [[Category:Yoshino and Ōmine]]

Within the precincts remains a dance mound said to mark the spot where {{ill|lt=Shizuka Gozen|Q3088217|WD=Q3088217|1=Shizuka Gozen}}, the wife of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Q310445|WD=Q310445|1=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}}, performed a dance when she was captured by pursuers. The {{ill|lt=main hall|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}}, built in the {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style with a {{ill|lt=hiwada-buki|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}} roof, measuring eight bays by two bays, was designated a prefectural {{ill|lt=tangible cultural property|Q2901860|WD=Q2901860|1=Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)}}. However, it was destroyed by suspected arson in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=2001|Q1988|WD=Q1988|1=2001}}). The sacred object has since been transferred to the facing {{ill|lt=Yoshimizu Shrine|Q3572667|WD=Q3572667|1=Yoshimizu Shrine}}, and donations are being solicited for the reconstruction of the main hall. === Relationship with Zao Gongen and Komori Myōjin === {{Multiple image | footer = Katte Myōjin (left) and Komori Myōjin (right) as depicted in the Yoshino Mandala | image1 = Katte-Myojin.jpg | image2 = Komori-Myojin.jpg | total_width = 350 }} {{ill|lt=Zao Gongen|Q10514059|WD=Q10514059|1=Zaō Gongen}} ({{ill|lt=Kinpusen-ji|Q1741668|WD=Q1741668|1=Kimpusen-ji}}), Komori Gongen (Yoshino Mikumari Shrine), and Katte Gongen (Katte Shrine) were enshrined together as the Three Gongen at {{ill|lt=Mount Mitoku’s Sanbutsu-ji|Q1053703|WD=Q1053703|lt=Sanbutsu-ji in Hōki Province|1=Hōki Province}}. Zao Gongen was enshrined at the inner sanctuary ({{ill|lt=Nageire-dō|Q19826408|WD=Q19826408|1=Nageire-dō}}), Komori Gongen at the Jizō Hall, and Katte Gongen at the Monju Hall{{Cite web|Japanese book|date=2007-12-26| url=https://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/224955/02-1bunkazaiseirihyou.pdf | title=“Proposal for Assets Listed on the Tentative List of World Heritage Sites”: Cultural Properties Included in the Assets (5-1) Inventory Table | work=Official Tottori Prefecture Website | accessdate=2013-04-15}}. Katte Myōjin was also enshrined independently at shrines throughout the country and serves as the head shrine of 28 Katte Shrines nationwide{{Cite web|Japanese book| url=http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com/pdf/syuisho.pdf | title=Prospectus for Support of Katte Shrine Reconstruction | work=Official Website of Yoshimizu Shrine | accessdate=2013-04-10|archive-url=https://www.web.archive.org/web/20060219194625/http://www.yoshimizu-shrine.com:80/pdf/syuisho.pdf |archive-date=2006-02-19 |url-status=dead |url-status-date=2024-09-21}}. Komori Myōjin, the enshrined deity of Yoshino Mikumari Shrine, is said to be the consort deity of Katte Myōjin, with Katte Myōjin as the male deity and Komori Myōjin as the female deity. In the Noh play “{{ill|lt=Arashiyama (Noh)|Q17988500|WD=Q17988500|lt=Arashiyama|1=Arashiyama}},” which was established in the late Muromachi period, an elderly couple who serve as guardians of the cherry blossoms of {{ill|lt=Arashiyama|Q2859566|WD=Q2859566|1=Arashiyama}}—transplanted from Yoshino—are revealed to be manifestations of the deities Katte and Komori. The play’s plot proclaims that Zao Gongen, Katte Myōjin, and Komori Myōjin are three aspects of a single divine entity{{Cite web || url=http://www.tessen.org/dictionary/explain/arashiyama/ | title=Arashiyama | work=Tessenkai Noh Theatre Dictionary | accessdate=2013-04-09}}. {{-}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Katte-jinja (Yoshino, Nara)}} {{Osm box|n|2725147868}} * [https://www.kattejinja.com/ Katte Shrine Reconstruction Committee] {{Shinto}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=Nara Prefecture}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Normdaten}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Katte Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Katte Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Yoshino]] [[Category:History of Yoshino]] [[Category:Yoshino and Ōmine]]
